atlas.name: Zika virus disease
atlas.subtitle: Aedes ecology, Pacific jumps, pregnancy surveillance, and sexual transmission
atlas.summary: A mosquito-borne flavivirus whose modern importance comes from congenital risk, urban Aedes habitat, travel, and sexual transmission.
atlas.why_it_matters: It forces the atlas to keep vector ecology and reproductive epidemiology in the same frame.
atlas.atlas_scope: Aedes mosquito expansion, pregnancy risk, and the 2015-2016 congenital-malformation crisis

origin_claim.label: Zika Forest / East African discovery frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First identified in Uganda in 1947; later urban and transoceanic expansion
origin_claim.narrative: The cautious origin frame is East African discovery and sylvatic Aedes-primate ecology, not a single clean birthplace for modern Zika.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Mid twentieth century recognition beyond Africa
spread_routes.narrative: Serology and outbreaks moved Zika from a sylvatic curiosity into a wider Old World arbovirus map.
Island outbreaks preceded explosive American spread, where congenital outcomes changed the political meaning of an arbovirus map.

modern_echoes: Modern Zika coverage still turns on travel, pregnancy counseling, mosquito suitability, and local transmission evidence.
It shows how a mild infection can become historically important through one exposed subgroup.
framing_traps: Do not treat Zika as just another dengue-like fever; congenital risk changed the surveillance problem.
Do not map Aedes presence as identical to sustained Zika transmission.

citations.claim_supported: Aedes transmission, sylvatic and urban cycles, and congenital/sexual transmission.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: WHO Western Pacific. Zika virus fact sheet. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Pregnancy risk, PHEIC history, and persistent low-level transmission.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Chikungunya
atlas.subtitle: A fever-and-joint-pain arbovirus that follows Aedes habitat and immunologically naive populations
atlas.summary: A mosquito-borne alphavirus whose spread tracks Aedes ecology, container water, travel, viral adaptation, and explosive outbreaks.
atlas.why_it_matters: It shows how mosquito ecology, viral adaptation, and human movement can rapidly change a disease map.
atlas.atlas_scope: Aedes urbanization from East Africa into Asia, islands, Europe, and the Americas

origin_claim.label: Tanzania / East African discovery setting
origin_claim.date_or_era: First identified in Tanzania in 1952
origin_claim.narrative: The first recognized chikungunya outbreak was in what is now Tanzania, followed by African and Asian circulation and wider Aedes-driven expansion.

spread_routes.narrative: Early recognized spread moved the virus into urban Asian outbreak settings.
Travel, viral adaptation, and Aedes albopictus/aegypti distributions helped widen outbreak risk.

modern_echoes: Chikungunya now sits beside dengue and Zika in the diagnostic problem of overlapping Aedes-borne fever.
New vaccines make it a moving target for travel medicine and outbreak preparedness.
framing_traps: Do not reduce chikungunya to dengue with joint pain; prolonged arthralgia changes burden.
Do not imply that every Aedes region has equal current outbreak risk.

citations.claim_supported: First identification in Tanzania, Aedes transmission, and spread to more than 110 countries.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Clinical and public-health framing for chikungunya surveillance and prevention.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: West Nile virus
atlas.subtitle: A Culex-bird virus that turned into a North American seasonal surveillance system
atlas.summary: West Nile maps best as an ecology of birds, Culex mosquitoes, horses, humans, wetlands, cities, and seasonal heat.
atlas.why_it_matters: It gives the atlas a clean ecological counterexample to human-centered epidemic maps.
atlas.atlas_scope: Bird-mosquito ecology, dead-end mammal hosts, and rapid North American establishment

origin_claim.label: West Nile district, Uganda discovery setting
origin_claim.date_or_era: First isolated in Uganda in 1937
origin_claim.narrative: The name and earliest isolation anchor the virus in Uganda, but the practical atlas frame is the bird-mosquito cycle.

spread_routes.narrative: West Nile became visible as a recurring bird-mosquito encephalitis signal across Afro-Eurasian wetlands and cities.
After the 1999 New York outbreak, bird-mosquito transmission allowed the virus to establish across much of North America.

modern_echoes: Modern surveillance depends on mosquito pools, bird ecology, horse cases, weather, and neuroinvasive disease reporting.
The North American story is a rare modern example of continent-scale establishment visible within years.
framing_traps: Do not map human cases as the reservoir; humans and horses are usually dead-end hosts.
Do not treat mosquito-borne diseases as one interchangeable category.

citations.short_citation: CDC. Transmission of West Nile Virus. 2024.
citations.claim_supported: Culex-bird cycle, dead-end human and horse hosts, and rare non-mosquito routes.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: CDC. West Nile: Causes and How It Spreads. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: West Nile as a flavivirus spread primarily by mosquitoes after feeding on infected birds.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Japanese encephalitis
atlas.subtitle: Mosquitoes, pigs, water birds, rice landscapes, and childhood immunity
atlas.summary: Japanese encephalitis turns the atlas toward agricultural ecology: Culex mosquitoes, pigs, water birds, rice cultivation, and vaccination.
atlas.why_it_matters: It is an atlas entry where land use and animal husbandry are the map, not background context.
atlas.atlas_scope: Rice agriculture, pigs, water birds, and Culex transmission across Asia

origin_claim.label: Japan / early documented clinical frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First documented disease case in Japan in 1871
origin_claim.narrative: The earliest documented clinical frame is Japan; endemic transmission now belongs to a wider South-East Asian and Western Pacific agricultural ecology.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Late nineteenth to twentieth century recognition
spread_routes.narrative: Clinical recognition in Japan led into a wider Asian encephalitis map linked to Culex mosquitoes and amplifying hosts.
Rice agriculture, pigs, water birds, and seasonal mosquito abundance sustain modern risk geography.

modern_echoes: Vaccination policy is inseparable from geography because risk is concentrated where enzootic transmission is recognized.
Climate, rice cultivation, and pig husbandry keep this disease in the atlas as an ecology problem.
framing_traps: Do not confuse Japanese encephalitis with direct human-to-human spread.
Do not treat Asia as homogeneous; risk varies with ecology, season, vaccination, and exposure.

citations.claim_supported: Culex transmission, pig/water bird cycle, Asian distribution, and vaccine prevention.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Regional public-health framing and severity in the Western Pacific.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Poliomyelitis
atlas.subtitle: An eradication-era virus shaped by immunity gaps, sanitation, and surveillance
atlas.summary: Polio is now a map of fecal-oral transmission, under-immunization, environmental surveillance, and eradication politics.
atlas.why_it_matters: It turns the atlas toward the geography of eradication, not only emergence.
atlas.atlas_scope: Fecal-oral transmission, eradication, and the infrastructure of surveillance

origin_claim.label: Modern endemic Afghanistan-Pakistan frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Ancient disease with modern endemic strongholds in Afghanistan and Pakistan
origin_claim.narrative: Deep origin is not usefully reduced to one point; the practical frame is modern persistence in remaining endemic and vaccine-derived transmission zones.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Late twentieth to twenty-first century endemic persistence
spread_routes.narrative: The remaining wild-poliovirus map is shaped by mobility, conflict, vaccination access, and environmental detection.
Mass vaccination and surveillance collapsed the global wild-polio map from more than 125 endemic countries to final hard-to-reach zones.

modern_echoes: Polio remains a daily lesson that “almost eradicated” is still geographically fragile.
Wastewater and acute flaccid paralysis surveillance make invisible transmission visible.
framing_traps: Do not imply wild poliovirus origin is the same as present-day endemic persistence.
Do not ignore vaccine-derived poliovirus in modern polio geography.

citations.claim_supported: Fecal-oral transmission, >99% reduction since 1988, remaining endemic countries, and eradication framing.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Global Polio Eradication Initiative and vaccine-preventable disease context.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Hepatitis A
atlas.subtitle: A fecal-oral virus that maps sanitation, food systems, travel, and adult susceptibility
atlas.summary: Hepatitis A shows how improving sanitation can shift symptomatic disease into older, more vulnerable age groups.
atlas.why_it_matters: It adds a disease where modernization changes who gets sick, not simply how much disease exists.
atlas.atlas_scope: Food, water, sanitation, travel, and cyclic outbreak recurrence

origin_claim.label: Global sanitation-gradient frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Worldwide fecal-oral infection; modern endemicity follows sanitation gradients
origin_claim.narrative: The useful frame is a worldwide fecal-oral virus whose disease burden changes with childhood exposure, sanitation, vaccination, and travel.

spread_routes.narrative: Sanitation transitions can move populations from early childhood infection toward outbreaks among susceptible adolescents and adults.
Food, water, and close-contact outbreaks can move hepatitis A into settings where adult immunity gaps exist.

modern_echoes: Large adult outbreaks in high-income countries often reveal homelessness, drug-use networks, food contamination, or travel.
It bridges sanitation history and modern vaccine policy.
framing_traps: Do not treat hepatitis A like hepatitis B or C; it does not produce chronic infection.
Do not map endemicity as moral failure; it is infrastructure, exposure age, and vaccine access.

citations.claim_supported: Fecal-oral transmission, sanitation gradient, food/water outbreaks, and vaccine prevention.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: CDC. Clinical Overview of Hepatitis A. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Clinical and public-health overview including fecal-oral transmission and vaccination.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Typhoid fever
atlas.subtitle: A Salmonella Typhi map of unsafe water, food systems, carriers, and resistant lineages
atlas.summary: Typhoid follows infrastructure: water safety, sanitation, food handling, urban crowding, carriers, and antibiotic pressure.
atlas.why_it_matters: It connects nineteenth-century sanitation lessons to twenty-first-century antimicrobial resistance.
atlas.atlas_scope: Water, sanitation, antimicrobial resistance, and urban enteric disease

origin_claim.label: South Asian high-burden modern frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Ancient human-adapted enteric disease; modern burden concentrated where water and sanitation are weak
origin_claim.narrative: The deep history is human and old, but the modern frame is high burden in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa with antimicrobial resistance.

spread_routes.narrative: Unsafe drinking water and inadequate sanitation sustain high-burden transmission across urban and periurban settings.
International travel makes typhoid a local water problem and a global antimicrobial-resistance signal.

modern_echoes: Typhoid conjugate vaccines are changing prevention strategy in high-burden countries.
The disease is a quiet index of water infrastructure and antibiotic pressure.
framing_traps: Do not make typhoid a purely historical disease.
Do not confuse typhoid fever with typhus.

citations.claim_supported: Food/water transmission, burden, vaccine prevention, and antimicrobial-resistance concern.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: CDC Yellow Book. Typhoid and Paratyphoid Fever.
citations.claim_supported: Travel medicine and enteric fever risk framing.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Norovirus
atlas.subtitle: A confined-space gastroenteritis virus that turns surfaces, food, water, and vomiting into a transmission map
atlas.summary: Norovirus is less a single-route disease than a choreography of people, surfaces, food, water, aerosolized vomitus, and institutional crowding.
atlas.why_it_matters: It adds the disease ecology of closed rooms, hand hygiene, shellfish beds, and travel infrastructure.
atlas.atlas_scope: Cruise ships, institutions, food service, shellfish, and extreme environmental persistence

origin_claim.label: Norwalk, Ohio discovery frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Norwalk, Ohio outbreak investigation in 1968; virus identified in 1972
origin_claim.narrative: The discovery frame begins with the Norwalk school outbreak, but the modern geography is global and dominated by crowded settings and food-water interfaces.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Late twentieth-century recognition and global syndrome
spread_routes.narrative: Laboratory methods made norovirus visible across schools, hospitals, ships, and care facilities.
Shellfish, food handlers, contaminated water, and confined populations create maritime, institutional, and household outbreaks.

modern_echoes: Norovirus is one of the cleanest examples of why surfaces and food handlers still matter.
It is the atlas entry for density, institutions, and environmental persistence.
framing_traps: Do not call norovirus “stomach flu”; it is not influenza.
Do not treat cruise ships as the reservoir; they are amplification settings.

citations.claim_supported: Spread through people, contaminated food/water/surfaces, vomit/fecal particles, and post-illness contagiousness.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Fecal-oral, food/water, fomites, vomitus aerosols, and cruise/camp/dorm/care-facility outbreaks.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Smallpox
atlas.subtitle: A human-only orthopoxvirus from ancient trade routes to eradication surveillance
atlas.summary: Smallpox is the disease where historical spread and modern public health ended in true eradication.
atlas.why_it_matters: It anchors the project: disease moving through civilization, then being removed by surveillance and vaccination.
atlas.atlas_scope: Trade routes, empire, variolation, vaccination, and eradication

origin_claim.label: Ancient Afro-Eurasian evidence frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: At least 3000 years old; exact origin unknown
origin_claim.narrative: The origin is unknown; ancient Egyptian, Chinese, Indian, and Asian Minor evidence should be treated as early frames rather than a single birthplace.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Sixth century East Asian trade route spread
spread_routes.narrative: Increased trade with China and Korea is part of the classic narrative for smallpox entering Japan.
European colonization carried smallpox into immunologically vulnerable American populations.
Surveillance, ring vaccination, and field logistics pushed smallpox to eradication certification.

modern_echoes: Smallpox now survives as preparedness, laboratory, and biosecurity problem rather than a naturally circulating disease.
It is the gold-standard comparison for every eradication claim.
framing_traps: Do not claim a precise smallpox origin.
Do not make eradication look inevitable; it was a logistical and political achievement.

citations.claim_supported: Unknown origin, ancient evidence, global spread through trade/exploration, and eradication history.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Transmission, fatality, Jenner vaccine, 1967 intensified campaign, last natural case, and 1980 eradication.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Seasonal influenza
atlas.subtitle: A respiratory virus map of winter epidemics, tropical persistence, vaccination, and crowded indoor transmission
atlas.summary: Seasonal influenza is a recurrent global circulation system shaped by immunity, seasonality, travel, and indoor crowding.
atlas.why_it_matters: It gives the atlas a disease where recurrence, not novelty, is the point.
atlas.atlas_scope: Global respiratory seasonality, antigenic drift, and recurring institutional burden

origin_claim.label: Global seasonal-circulation frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Ancient respiratory syndrome; modern virologic surveillance is global
origin_claim.narrative: The useful frame is not a single origin point but the global respiratory network through which influenza viruses repeatedly circulate and evolve.

spread_routes.narrative: In temperate regions influenza concentrates into winter waves that pressure schools, workplaces, clinics, and hospitals.
In tropical regions influenza can circulate throughout the year, making seasonality more irregular.

modern_echoes: Influenza is a standing lesson in why schools, nursing homes, hospitals, and vaccination campaigns are geographic systems.
Its routine nature hides how large the annual burden is.
framing_traps: Do not collapse seasonal influenza into pandemic influenza.
Do not imply tropical influenza follows the same winter pattern as temperate influenza.

citations.claim_supported: Global burden, respiratory spread, temperate winter epidemics, tropical year-round circulation, and vaccination.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Seasonal influenza overview and surveillance resources.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: COVID-19 / SARS-CoV-2
atlas.subtitle: A pandemic coronavirus whose map turned air, buildings, borders, surveillance, and inequality into one disease surface
atlas.summary: COVID-19 made respiratory transmission visible as infrastructure: indoor air, work, travel, households, health systems, and variant surveillance.
atlas.why_it_matters: It is the modern respiratory atlas entry par excellence.
atlas.atlas_scope: Pandemic respiratory spread, travel networks, indoor air, and year-round circulation

origin_claim.label: Wuhan early-detection frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First detected as a cluster in Wuhan, China in late 2019; ultimate origin remains debated
origin_claim.narrative: The earliest recognized outbreak was in Wuhan in late 2019; the ultimate animal-origin pathway remains unsettled and should not be flattened into certainty.

spread_routes.narrative: Travel and dense indoor transmission moved SARS-CoV-2 from a local cluster into a pandemic respiratory network.
Variants made the map dynamic: immune escape, transmissibility, sequencing capacity, and reporting shaped apparent spread.

modern_echoes: COVID changed how readers understand dashboards, wastewater, genomic surveillance, and indoor air.
It is now a year-round circulation problem rather than a closed historical episode.
framing_traps: Do not state the ultimate origin as settled.
Do not reduce COVID transmission to surfaces; respiratory particles and indoor air are central.

citations.claim_supported: Airborne respiratory-particle transmission, year-round circulation, symptoms, and public-health framing.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: General SARS-CoV-2 overview and prevention framing.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Ebola disease
atlas.subtitle: A filovirus map built from wildlife contact, body fluids, health systems, burial practice, and borders
atlas.summary: Ebola shows how zoonotic spillover becomes a social and institutional transmission crisis.
atlas.why_it_matters: It maps social practice and health-system capacity without pretending the virus moves by geography alone.
atlas.atlas_scope: Forest spillover, caregiving, burial, hospitals, and regional borders

origin_claim.label: Central African first-recognition frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First recognized outbreaks in 1976 near Ebola River and Nzara/Yambuku contexts
origin_claim.narrative: The discovery frame is Central Africa in 1976; reservoir ecology is more complex than one river or village.

spread_routes.narrative: Initial recognition involved severe outbreaks where hospitals amplified transmission under difficult infection-control conditions.
Caregiving, burials, weak health systems, roads, and borders turned spillover into a regional emergency.

modern_echoes: Ebola tests whether outbreak maps include hospitals, funerals, trust, roads, and borders.
Vaccines changed response, but only for some Ebola viruses.
framing_traps: Do not imply people transmit before symptoms.
Do not make fruit bats a solved, single-source explanation for every outbreak.

citations.claim_supported: Animal spillover, body-fluid transmission, burial and health-care transmission, and outbreak control.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: WHO. Ebola virus disease health topic.
citations.claim_supported: 1976 first outbreaks and 2014-2016 West African spread.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Marburg virus disease
atlas.subtitle: A Rousettus-bat filovirus whose map begins in caves and mines, then moves through care networks
atlas.summary: Marburg starts with bat habitats and occupational exposure, then becomes a body-fluid and hospital transmission problem.
atlas.why_it_matters: It adds an occupational/ecological filovirus map distinct from Ebola.
atlas.atlas_scope: Caves, mines, fruit bats, laboratories, and filovirus response

origin_claim.label: Marburg/Frankfurt/Belgrade laboratory-recognition frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First detected in 1967 in Germany and Serbia after imported African green monkey exposure
origin_claim.narrative: First detected in European laboratory outbreaks linked to imported African green monkeys; later geography ties to African bat reservoirs and caves/mines.

spread_routes.narrative: The first recognized outbreak made a Central/East African filovirus visible in Europe.

spread_routes.to_label: Central and southern African outbreak settings
spread_routes.date_or_era: Modern African cave and mine exposure
spread_routes.narrative: Later outbreaks point back to cave and mine exposure followed by body-fluid transmission.

modern_echoes: Marburg response turns on recognition, infection control, safe burial, and occupational exposure histories.
The 1967 story shows laboratories and animal trade can reveal pathogens far from reservoirs.
framing_traps: Do not treat Marburg and Ebola as the same virus with different names.
Do not omit cave/mine exposure ecology.

citations.short_citation: WHO. Marburg virus disease fact sheet. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: Rousettus fruit bat host, cave/mine exposure, direct body-fluid transmission, and fatality range.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: WHO. Marburg virus disease health topic.
citations.claim_supported: 1967 laboratory recognition and later African outbreak geography.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Nipah virus disease
atlas.subtitle: A henipavirus map of flying foxes, pig farms, raw sap, and close-contact transmission
atlas.summary: Nipah has multiple routes: bat-contaminated food, pig-amplified farm outbreaks, and limited person-to-person spread.
atlas.why_it_matters: It forces the atlas to track food habits, farms, bats, hospitals, and household care in one entry.
atlas.atlas_scope: Fruit bats, pigs, date-palm sap, hospitals, and South/Southeast Asian spillover

origin_claim.label: Malaysia pig-farm discovery frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First identified during Malaysia/Singapore pig-farm outbreak in 1998-1999
origin_claim.narrative: First recognized during pig-farm outbreaks in Malaysia and Singapore; later Bangladesh and India outbreaks emphasized date palm sap and close contact.

spread_routes.narrative: Pig amplification moved a bat-associated virus into farmers and abattoir workers.
Raw date palm sap contamination, bat ecology, and close caregiving define the South Asian Nipah map.

modern_echoes: Nipah is a pandemic-watchlist pathogen because it combines high severity with some human-to-human spread.
The prevention map is partly agricultural and food-practice geography.
framing_traps: Do not claim Nipah is easily airborne.
Do not make all Nipah outbreaks pig-farm outbreaks.

citations.claim_supported: Fruit bat reservoir, first recognition, outbreak countries, food contamination, animal contact, and person-to-person spread.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Fruit bat reservoir, outbreak countries, pig outbreak history, and high fatality framing.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Lassa fever
atlas.subtitle: A Mastomys-rat arenavirus whose map is home, food storage, farms, and hospitals
atlas.summary: Lassa is domestic and ecological at once: rodents, stored food, household contamination, pregnancy risk, and health-care infection control.
atlas.why_it_matters: It adds a quieter map of household ecology and health-system vulnerability.
atlas.atlas_scope: West African rodent ecology, household food storage, and health-care transmission

origin_claim.label: Nigeria first-identification frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First identified in Nigeria in 1969
origin_claim.narrative: Lassa was first identified in Nigeria; its modern endemic frame sits across parts of West Africa where Mastomys rodents live near homes.

spread_routes.narrative: Mastomys ecology and household food contamination define the endemic belt.
Blood and body-fluid exposure can amplify transmission in health-care settings without adequate infection control.

modern_echoes: Lassa shows how food storage and domestic architecture can be epidemiologic infrastructure.
Pregnancy and hospital infection control make its burden larger than raw case counts imply.
framing_traps: Do not describe Lassa as primarily airborne.
Do not treat West Africa as uniform risk.

citations.claim_supported: Mastomys reservoir, West African endemicity, food/household contamination, and health-care risk.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Endemic countries, fatality framing, and prompt treatment importance.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Rabies
atlas.subtitle: A fatal zoonosis whose map is dogs, bites, vaccination, and access to PEP
atlas.summary: Rabies geography determines whether a bite is survivable: dog vaccination, wound care, immunoglobulin, and vaccine access are the real map.
atlas.why_it_matters: It adds a One Health entry where veterinary public health and human mortality are inseparable.
atlas.atlas_scope: Dog-mediated zoonosis, bite exposure, post-exposure prophylaxis, and One Health

origin_claim.label: Modern dog-mediated Africa/Asia burden frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Ancient zoonosis; modern deaths concentrated in Africa and Asia
origin_claim.narrative: Rabies has an ancient history, but the atlas frame is modern dog-mediated transmission and uneven access to post-exposure prophylaxis.

spread_routes.narrative: Dog bites and scratches drive the overwhelming majority of human rabies cases.
The same exposure has radically different outcomes depending on wound washing, vaccine, immunoglobulin, and clinical access.

modern_echoes: Rabies is access-to-care geography: fatal after symptoms, preventable after exposure.
Dog vaccination is human public health.
framing_traps: Do not imply rabies is treatable after clinical symptoms.
Do not center bats globally when dog-mediated rabies causes most human deaths.

citations.claim_supported: Dog bites/scratches cause 99% of human rabies cases; fatality after symptoms; PEP and One Health prevention.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Estimated annual deaths, Africa/Asia concentration, and dog-mediated prevention.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Anthrax
atlas.subtitle: A spore-forming bacterium whose map is soil, grazing animals, animal products, and disturbed landscapes
atlas.summary: Anthrax is environmental memory: spores persist in soil, livestock encounter them, and humans meet the pathogen through animals and animal products.
atlas.why_it_matters: It gives the atlas a disease where the landscape itself stores risk.
atlas.atlas_scope: Soil spores, livestock, hides, wool, meat, and occupational exposure

origin_claim.label: Global livestock-soil ecology frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Worldwide soil ecology with recurrent livestock-associated outbreaks
origin_claim.narrative: The meaningful geography is persistence of Bacillus anthracis spores in soils that expose grazing animals and people who handle them.

spread_routes.narrative: Animals become infected through contaminated soil, plants, or water.
Hides, wool, meat, and contaminated animal products can move risk from rural ecology into workplaces and trade networks.

modern_echoes: Anthrax reveals drought, grazing, soil disruption, livestock vaccination, and occupational safety.
It explains environmental persistence without mysticism.
framing_traps: Do not make anthrax only a bioterrorism story.
Do not imply person-to-person spread drives ordinary anthrax outbreaks.

citations.claim_supported: Soil spore ecology, livestock/wildlife infection, and human exposure through animals or animal products.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.claim_supported: Naturally occurring soil distribution and inhalation, ingestion, or cutaneous exposure routes.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Middle East respiratory syndrome
atlas.subtitle: A camel coronavirus whose map is zoonotic exposure plus health-care amplification
atlas.summary: MERS shows how a poorly efficient human transmitter can still cause serious hospital outbreaks when triage and infection control fail.
atlas.why_it_matters: It is a compact map of zoonotic exposure, travel medicine, and hospital preparedness.
atlas.atlas_scope: Dromedary camels, Arabian Peninsula spillover, hospitals, and travel-linked outbreaks

origin_claim.label: Arabian Peninsula first-recognition frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: First identified in Saudi Arabia and Jordan in 2012
origin_claim.narrative: MERS-CoV was first identified in Saudi Arabia and Jordan, with dromedary camels as the primary reservoir for zoonotic transmission.

spread_routes.narrative: Camel contact and raw camel products anchor the recurring zoonotic transmission map.
One travel-linked case spread through hospitals before recognition and isolation.

modern_echoes: MERS warns that health-care systems can amplify diseases that do not spread efficiently in ordinary community settings.
Camel economies and hospital infection control belong on the same map.
framing_traps: Do not imply sustained global human-to-human transmission.
Do not erase camels from the story by making it just another coronavirus.

citations.short_citation: WHO. Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus fact sheet. 2025.
citations.claim_supported: First identification, camel reservoir, Arabian Peninsula concentration, Korea hospital outbreak, and transmission.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

citations.short_citation: CDC. About Middle East Respiratory Syndrome. 2024.
citations.claim_supported: Camel-to-human and limited human-to-human spread, health-care outbreaks, and prevention advice.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Diphtheria
atlas.subtitle: A toxin-producing respiratory bacterium that returns where immunization and living conditions break down
atlas.summary: Diphtheria is a vaccine-preventable disease whose geography reappears through under-immunization, displacement, and damaged health systems.
atlas.why_it_matters: It shows that old vaccine-preventable diseases are not gone; they are suppressed by systems.
atlas.atlas_scope: Vaccination gaps, crowding, respiratory droplets, toxin, and conflict-disrupted health services

origin_claim.label: Global pre-vaccine and under-immunized resurgence frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Historically worldwide before vaccination; modern outbreaks follow immunity gaps
origin_claim.narrative: The useful frame is a historically global respiratory infection that resurges where vaccine coverage and boosters fail.

spread_routes.narrative: Before vaccination, diphtheria belonged to childhood, crowding, and respiratory spread.
Disrupted routine immunization and crowded living conditions can reopen old disease maps.

modern_echoes: Diphtheria is a marker of routine immunization failure.
It connects refugee camps, conflict, primary care, and booster policy.
framing_traps: Do not write diphtheria as a disease of the past.
Do not forget toxin-mediated severity.

citations.claim_supported: Respiratory transmission, toxin disease, vaccination, under-immunization, and outbreak resurgence.
citations.note: Official source used for transmission, geography, or public-health framing.

atlas.name: Pertussis
atlas.subtitle: A cough disease whose map is babies, households, schools, boosters, and diagnostic visibility
atlas.summary: Pertussis vaccination changed but did not erase the disease; waning immunity and infant vulnerability keep it clinically alive.
atlas.why_it_matters: It gives the atlas a disease where controlled is not the same as eliminated.
atlas.atlas_scope: Respiratory droplets, infant risk, waning immunity, and vaccine-era recurrence

origin_claim.label: Global respiratory childhood-disease frame
origin_claim.date_or_era: Historically worldwide; modern recurrence shaped by vaccination and waning immunity
origin_claim.narrative: Pertussis is best mapped as a historically global respiratory infection whose modern geography depends on vaccination, boosters, diagnosis, and infant protection.

spread_routes.narrative: Older children and adults with mild or missed disease can transmit into households where infants face highest risk.
Pertussis persists through waning immunity, diagnostic changes, and pockets of under-vaccination.

modern_echoes: Pertussis is a household-network disease disguised as a childhood cough.
Infant protection depends on the immunity of everyone around them.
framing_traps: Do not imply pertussis vaccination provides permanent sterilizing immunity.
Do not focus only on classic whoop; adult and adolescent disease may be missed.

citations.claim_supported: Droplet spread, infant risk, global cases, symptoms, contagious period, and immunization prevention.
